contract.description: Focus on mistakes, patterns, or non-obvious insights.

selection.selected_title: What Steve Yegge’s Google story gets right (and wrong) about how big companies adopt AI
selection.selected_reason: It’s a live conversation about real AI adoption pain inside one of the most competent engineering orgs on the planet. Perfect hook to talk about the non-obvious patterns I’m seeing in enterprises trying to move from "play with LLMs" to "deploy AI systems". I can use his story as a foil to: (1) highlight what people systematically get wrong (treating AI as a feature, not an operating change), (2) call out specific anti-patterns (committee-ware, platform worship, fake experiments), and (3) contrast it with what actually works when you’re building agents and AI workflows in production. It’s timely, opinionated, and lets me speak like a practitioner, not a pundit.
backup_titles: LLMs are not lazy – but your AI architecture probably is (riffing on Bryan Cantrill’s point)
Notion’s ‘Token Town’ vs your AI roadmap: what people copy, what they miss, and why most internal agents flop
caution_notes: Don’t just summarize Steve Yegge’s conversation; use it as a jumping-off point to present my own concrete patterns and hard-earned opinions.
Avoid culture-warting Google or turning it into a gossip post; the authority comes from dissecting patterns that apply to any large org, not from dunking on one company.
Make at least one slide uncomfortably specific (e.g., a real anti-pattern I’ve seen in AI platform teams) so it doesn’t read like second-hand commentary.
